docs.title: Autonomous Logistics Systems and IoT
docs.text:
Welcome to the self-study companion for the Master's course Autonomous Logistics Systems and IoT at OTH Amberg-Weiden.

About this site

This site contains self-study material, assignment descriptions, and reference content for all course blocks. It accompanies the live sessions — not a replacement for attending class.

docs.title: Course Structure
docs.text: Block Topic Format Block 1 Organisation & AGV Foundations Lecture + student research Block 2 Classical AGVs with Webots Hands-on simulation Block 3 Autonomous AGVs with ROS Demo + guided activities Block 4 AGV Simulation Plant Simulation assignment Assessment Business Case Semester break submission

docs.title: Assessment
docs.text: Component Weight Timing In-semester work (Blocks 2–4) 70 % During semester Business Case 30 % Semester break

docs.title: Course Materials
docs.text:
All assignment sheets, code templates, and simulation files are available in the GitHub repository.

Lecture slides are distributed via Moodle.

docs.title: Assessment — Business Case
docs.text:
Weight: 30 % · Submission: semester break

Template coming soon

The business case template and grading rubric are being prepared and will be published here.

docs.title: Overview
docs.text: You will develop a structured business case for the introduction of an AGV system in an intralogistics scenario.

docs.title: Components
docs.text: Component Description Market research Overview of available AGV systems, vendors, and market trends Business case calculation ROI, payback period, TCO, cost-benefit analysis Requirements specification (Lastenheft) Structured procurement requirements Utility value analysis (Nutzwertanalyse) Weighted comparison of at least two AGV alternatives

docs.title: Block 1 — Organisation & Foundations
docs.text:
Sessions 1–2 · Duration: approx. 2 weeks

This block introduces the course structure and builds the theoretical foundation for all subsequent blocks. It covers the history, technology, and modern applications of Automated Guided Vehicles (AGVs).

docs.title: Learning Objectives
docs.text:
By the end of this block, you will be able to:

  • Describe what an AGV is and how it differs from other intralogistics systems
  • Explain the four historical epochs of AGV development
  • Name and explain the core technical components of an AGV system
  • Present industry-specific examples of AGV applications in your assigned sector

docs.title: Session 1 — Organisation
docs.text:
• Course structure, schedule, and assessment overview
  • Assignment of industry research topics

docs.title: Session 2 — AGV Fundamentals
docs.text: Lecturer presentation covering all foundation topics (see AGV Fundamentals for the self-study version).

docs.title: Student Assignment — Industry Research
docs.text:
Group task

Each group researches AGV applications in one specific industry sector and presents their findings to the class.

Your task:

  1. Research how AGVs are used in your assigned industry (e.g. automotive, e-commerce, healthcare, food & beverage)
  2. Identify at least two concrete application examples with real companies or systems
  3. Cover: use cases, technical requirements, and operational challenges
  4. Prepare a short presentation (approx. 10 minutes) for the class

Useful starting points: industry trade press, manufacturer websites (e.g. KION, Jungheinrich, Mobile Industrial Robots), and the course textbook (Ullrich & Albrecht, 2019).

docs.title: Textbook
docs.text:
The course is based on:

Ullrich, G., & Albrecht, T. (2019). Fahrerlose Transportsysteme: Eine Fibel — mit Praxisanwendungen — zur Technik — für die Planung (3rd ed.). Springer Vieweg.

Available through the OTH library.

docs.title: AGV Fundamentals
docs.text: This page summarises the theoretical content from Block 1 for self-study and revision.

docs.title: Definition
docs.text:
An Automated Guided Vehicle (AGV) — in German: Fahrerloses Transportsystem (FTS) — is defined by VDI guideline 2510 as:

An in-plant, floor-bound conveying system with automatically controlled vehicles, whose primary task is material transport — not the transport of persons.

Key characteristics:

  • Operates within a facility (intralogistics, not public roads)
  • Floor-bound movement (no aerial or rail systems)
  • Automatically controlled — no human driver
  • Designed for material transport

docs.title: Four Epochs of AGV Development
docs.text: Epoch Period Characteristic 1 ~1955–1975 Idea and early implementation; first inductively guided vehicles 2 ~1975–1995 Automation euphoria; rapid adoption in manufacturing 3 ~1995–2010 Established technology for intralogistics; standardisation 4 ~2010–today Classical AGVs plus innovative autonomous systems (AMRs)

docs.title: 1. Navigation & Guidance
docs.text:
How the vehicle knows where it is and where to go.

Method Principle Typical use Inductive Wire embedded in floor carries current; vehicle follows magnetic field Fixed routes, heavy industry Optical Vehicle follows a painted or taped line on the floor Simple, low-cost installations Laser (reflector-based) Laser scanner measures distance to fixed reflectors Flexible, reprogrammable routes Natural navigation SLAM — vehicle builds a map of its environment using sensors Modern AMRs; no infrastructure needed Magnetic spots Grid of magnets in floor; vehicle navigates by counting spots High precision in confined areas

docs.title: 2. Safety Systems
docs.text:
AGVs share space with people and must meet strict safety standards (EN ISO 3691-4).

  • Laser scanners (protective field): detect obstacles and trigger emergency stop
  • Bumpers: mechanical contact detection as a last resort
  • Warning lights and sound signals: alert people to the vehicle's presence and intentions
  • Speed reduction: vehicle slows in monitored zones, stops in protective zones

docs.title: 3. Fleet Management (Leitsteuerung)
docs.text:
The fleet management system coordinates multiple AGVs:

  • Assigns transport orders to available vehicles
  • Manages traffic (collision avoidance, intersection control)
  • Monitors battery status and dispatches vehicles for charging
  • Interfaces with warehouse management systems (WMS) and ERP

docs.title: 4. Vehicle Types
docs.text: Type Load handling Typical application Towing vehicle Tows trailers Long-distance transport in large facilities Unit load carrier Carries pallets, containers Warehouse and production Fork AGV Forks load from floor or rack High-bay warehouses Underride vehicle Slides under load carrier, lifts it E-commerce fulfilment Assembly AGV Carries product through assembly line Automotive manufacturing

docs.title: 5. Operating Environment
docs.text:
AGVs require a prepared environment:

  • Floor quality: smooth, level, sufficient load-bearing capacity
  • Navigation infrastructure: reflectors, markers, or mapped environment (depending on navigation type)
  • Traffic zones: clearly defined routes, intersections, waiting areas
  • Charging stations: opportunity charging (brief top-ups) or full charging bays

docs.title: 6. Trends and Future Developments
docs.text:
• Autonomous Mobile Robots (AMRs): natural navigation, dynamic obstacle avoidance, no fixed infrastructure
  • Collaborative operation: AGVs and humans working in shared spaces (cobots)
  • AI-based fleet optimisation: dynamic order prioritisation, predictive maintenance
  • Swarm intelligence: decentralised coordination without central controller
  • Integration with IoT: real-time data from vehicles fed into digital twin, WMS, ERP

docs.title: Key Distinction: Classical AGV vs. AMR
docs.text:
Classical AGV AMR (Autonomous Mobile Robot) Navigation Fixed infrastructure (wire, tape, reflectors) Natural navigation (SLAM) Route changes Requires physical changes to facility Software update only Obstacle handling Emergency stop Dynamic re-routing Cost Higher upfront (infrastructure) Higher unit cost, lower installation cost Flexibility Low High

Exam relevance

The AGV vs. AMR distinction frequently appears in business case analyses. Understanding when to choose which system is directly relevant to the course assessment.

docs.title: Block 2 — Classical AGVs with Webots
docs.text:
Sessions 3–8 · Duration: approx. 6 weeks

Content coming soon

Detailed self-study material for this block is being prepared. Assignment sheets and code templates are available in the GitHub repository.

docs.title: Assignments
docs.text:
Assignment Description Obstacle avoidance Model a robot; complete the obstacle avoidance controller template Labyrinth Complete a maze-solving controller; present results Line following Complete a line-tracking controller (basic + extended)

All assignments use pre-written code templates — you fill in the marked sections. No prior programming knowledge is required.

docs.title: Block 3 — Autonomous AGVs with ROS
docs.text:
Sessions 9–10 · Duration: approx. 2 weeks

Content coming soon

Detailed self-study material for this block is being prepared. Activity sheets and scripts are available in the GitHub repository.

docs.title: Units
docs.text: Unit Format ROS Fundamentals Lecturer demo + guided activity SLAM & Navigation Lecturer demo + Gazebo activity Manipulation LoCoBot demo + parameter modification activity

docs.title: Block 4 — AGV Simulation
docs.text:
Sessions 11–13 · Duration: approx. 3 weeks

Content coming soon

The assignment sheet and Plant Simulation model files are being prepared. They will be made available here and in the GitHub repository.

docs.title: Scenario
docs.text:
You will simulate a wave manufacturing production environment in two configurations:

  1. Classical layout — manual transport between stations
  2. AGV-supported layout — automated transport

Your task is to analyse and compare throughput, lead times, and bottlenecks.

Tool: Siemens Plant Simulation (available on the computer lab VMs)
